IBOR Implementation
Training Guide

Build a complete Investment Book of Record on the LUSID platform. 8 notebooks covering instrument enrichment, portfolio construction, transaction loading, holdings generation, corporate actions, valuation, and reconciliation. Run each notebook, verify the result, then move to the next.
Audience
Implementation Consultants
Last Updated
April 2026
Notebooks
8 (NB00 to NB07)
Prerequisite
EDM+ Training Pack Complete
0

How This Training Works

Read, Run, Verify, Repeat
🎯
Why IBOR Training Matters
Who Uses IBOR and Why

The Investment Book of Record (IBOR) is the central, real time view of an investment firm's positions. Unlike traditional end of day batch systems, IBOR calculates holdings on demand from the complete transaction history, enabling intraday position visibility and bitemporal queries.

Front Office (Portfolio Managers & Traders) use IBOR for real time position monitoring, pre trade compliance checks, and intraday cash availability. They need to know "what do I hold right now?" and "what will my position look like after this trade?" LUSID's on demand holdings calculation answers both instantly.

Middle Office (Operations & Risk) use IBOR for trade settlement tracking, corporate action processing, and position reconciliation against custodians. They are the primary consumers of the reconciliation workflows, lifecycle event processing (bond coupons, stock splits, maturities), and exception management. A missed corporate action or late booking is their problem to solve.

Back Office (Fund Accounting & Reporting) use IBOR as the foundation for NAV calculation, regulatory reporting, and performance attribution. The IBOR feeds into the Accounting Book of Record (ABOR) and ultimately into client and regulatory reports. Data quality in the IBOR directly determines the accuracy of everything downstream.

Technology & Data Teams use IBOR for system integration, data pipeline design, and API automation. They build the connections between OMS, EMS, custodian feeds, market data providers, and LUSID. Understanding the IBOR data model (scopes, portfolios, instruments, properties, transactions, holdings) is essential for designing robust integrations.

Implementation Consultants need fluency across all four perspectives. During client workshops, you will be asked to demonstrate position queries (front office), configure lifecycle events (middle office), explain reconciliation breaks (back office), and design data flows (technology). This training pack covers all of it.
Key principle: LUSID does not store holdings as a flat file. Holdings are always calculated dynamically from the transaction history using the movements engine. You can ask for a holding at any point in time, as at any audit timestamp.

Prerequisites

Before starting this guide, you must have completed the EDM+ Implementation Training Pack (NB00 through NB06). Your LUSID domain should already contain:

  • Property definitions across Instrument, Portfolio, Transaction, and LegalEntity domains
  • 305 mastered instruments (117 equities, 87 bonds, 75 ETFs, 4 equity options, 3 FX forwards, 19 PE instruments)
  • 10 transaction portfolios with loaded transactions
  • 226 legal entities with issuer relationship definitions
  • Quotes in the Quote Store (EOD prices and FX rates)
  • Luminesce inline property configurations and reporting queries
EDM+ Foundation Required
Do not attempt the IBOR guide without completing the EDM+ pack first. The instruments, properties, legal entities, and market data loaded in the EDM+ notebooks are the foundation that every IBOR notebook builds on. Incomplete reference data cascades into every position and P&L calculation.

⚠️ Authentication Setup

Before running any notebook, you need a secrets.json file in your JupyterHub working directory (/home/jovyan/). This file contains your LUSID API credentials.

Option A: Personal Access Token (Recommended for Training)

  1. In the LUSID web app, click your profile icon (top right)

    2. Click your profile icon (top right) to find Personal Access Tokens:

  2. Select Personal Access Tokens
  3. Click Create Token, give it a name (e.g. "IBOR Training"), and copy the token value
  4. Create a file called secrets.json in your JupyterHub root directory with this content:
{
    "api": {
        "tokenUrl": "https://YOUR-DOMAIN.lusid.com/oauth2/token",
        "lusidUrl": "https://YOUR-DOMAIN.lusid.com/api",
        "accessToken": "YOUR-PAT-TOKEN-HERE"
    }
}
PAT Expiry
Personal Access Tokens expire. If you get authentication errors after a period of time, generate a new PAT and update the accessToken field in secrets.json.

Option B: Client Credentials (Production)

For production or long running environments, use OAuth client credentials instead of a PAT:

{
    "api": {
        "tokenUrl": "https://YOUR-DOMAIN.lusid.com/oauth2/token",
        "lusidUrl": "https://YOUR-DOMAIN.lusid.com/api",
        "clientId": "YOUR-CLIENT-ID",
        "clientSecret": "YOUR-CLIENT-SECRET"
    }
}

Client credentials can be obtained from your platform administrator or from Identity → Applications in the LUSID web app.

pip Upgrade Notice — IGNORE THIS

When running the first cell of each notebook, you may see a message like:

[notice] A new release of pip is available: 24.2 → 26.1
[notice] To update, run: pip install --upgrade pip

This is completely harmless and should be ignored. It is a standard pip informational notice, not an error. Do NOT run the pip upgrade command. The SDK packages will install correctly regardless of the pip version.

What You Will Build

By the end of this guide, your LUSID domain will contain a fully operational IBOR with:

  • 12 to 15 new instruments with full native economic definitions (fixed rate bonds, floating rate notes, callable bonds, amortising bonds, convertible bonds, zero coupon bonds, interest rate swaps, and term deposits) that generate real cashflows and lifecycle events
  • A multi asset portfolio structure with groups, derived portfolios, and sub holding key segmentation
  • Configured transaction types with custom movement definitions
  • Hundreds of transactions across equities, bonds, swaps, and cash
  • Dynamic holdings demonstrating coupon payments, floating rate resets, and principal paydowns
  • Corporate action and lifecycle event processing (stock splits, dividends, bond calls, conversions)
  • A working valuation recipe with a market data rules and 5D quote interval lookback
  • A custodian reconciliation framework with break detection
Market Data Date Range
The training data covers January 2024 to June 2025 (18 months). When running valuations or viewing holdings in the web app, always set the effective date within this range. Recommended dates for training exercises:
  • 2024-01-31 — Early state, only initial purchases
  • 2024-06-30 — Mid year, all instruments purchased, some sells executed
  • 2024-09-30 — Primary valuation date (most complete data coverage)
  • 2025-03-31 — Year end + Q1, demonstrates price movement over time
  • 2025-06-30 — Latest available date
Equity and bond prices are loaded monthly. FX rates (GBP/USD, EUR/USD) are loaded daily (every business day). SOFR fixings are loaded quarterly. If a valuation returns "MarketResolverFailure", the effective date is likely outside this range.
How to Load and Run the Notebooks
  1. Download all files from this training pack
  2. Upload secrets.json to the JupyterHub root directory: /home/jovyan/
  3. Create a data/ folder in the root directory: /home/jovyan/data/
  4. Upload all CSV files into the data/ folder
  5. Upload all notebook files (NB00 through NB07) to the root directory
  6. Open NB00_IBOR_Instrument_Enrichment.ipynb
  7. Select the Python (SDK v3) kernel from the kernel selector (top right)
  8. Click Run All (or use Kernel → Restart & Run All) to execute all cells
  9. Wait for each notebook to complete before moving to the next one
  10. Run notebooks in strict order: NB00 → NB01 → NB02 → NB03 → NB04 → NB05 → NB06 → NB07

Each notebook installs its own dependencies (lusid-sdk, lumipy) in the first cell. This takes a few seconds on the first run.

Notebook Sequence

NotebookTitleWhat It BuildsDepends On

NB01Portfolio Structure & Entity HierarchyPortfolios, groups, derived portfolios, SHKsNB00

1

NB00: IBOR Instrument Enrichment & Market Data

Bridge the gap between reference data and cashflow generating instruments
💡
EDM vs IBOR Instrument Definitions

The EDM+ pack mastered 305 instruments using simple type definitions with properties (coupon rate, maturity date, face value stored as metadata). That is correct for a security master. But for IBOR, LUSID needs the full native economic definition (FlowConventions, DayCountConvention, StartDate, MaturityDate, CouponRate, Principal) so its cashflow engine can generate lifecycle events automatically. This notebook creates that richer instrument set.

The difference: A simple Bond with properties tells you "this bond has a 5% coupon". A fully defined Bond with FlowConventions tells LUSID "generate a coupon payment event every 6 months on the 15th, using Act/365 day count, modified following roll convention, on the GBP calendar".
▶️ Run NB00

In JupyterHub, open NB00_IBOR_Instrument_Enrichment.ipynb, select the Python (SDK v3) kernel, then click Run → Run All Cells.

Creates all instruments and loads 18 months of market data. This is the longest running notebook (~2 minutes).

NB00 IBOR Instrument Enrichment & Market Data Setup
Creates 12 to 15 instruments with full native economic definitions and loads their supporting market data. Reuses existing EDM+ instruments by LUID for the equity, ETF, and FX positions.
  1. Authenticate and connect to LUSID (SDK v3 kernel)
  2. Create 2 fixed rate vanilla bonds (Bond type) with full FlowConventions: one USD semi annual, one GBP annual
  3. Create 1 floating rate note (ComplexBond type) with IndexConvention referencing SOFR + 150bps spread, quarterly resets
  4. Create 1 callable bond (ComplexBond type) with a call schedule (callable at par after 2 years)
  5. Create 1 amortising/sinkable bond (ComplexBond type) with a sinking schedule for principal paydown
  6. Create 1 convertible bond (ComplexBond type) with conversion ratio to an existing equity
  7. Create 1 zero coupon bond (Bond type with coupon rate 0, discount instrument)
  8. Create 1 interest rate swap (InterestRateSwap type) with fixed vs SOFR floating legs
  9. Create 1 term deposit (TermDeposit type) with fixed rate and maturity
  10. Load EOD prices for all new instruments to the Quote Store
  11. Load SOFR fixing rates (for FRN and IRS resets)
  12. Load SOFR daily fixings for IRS and FRN resets (provider: Lusid)
  13. Load futures and options market data
  14. Verify all instruments via Lusid.Instrument Luminesce provider
  15. Verify all quotes via Lusid.Instrument.Quote Luminesce provider

Instrument Definitions Created

NameLUSID TypeKey FeatureMarket Data Required
US Treasury 4.5% 2029BondUSD semi annual, Act/Act ISDAEOD clean price
UK Gilt 3.75% 2031BondGBP annual, Act/365EOD clean price
SOFR + 150bps FRN 2028ComplexBondQuarterly resets, floating couponSOFR fixings, EOD price
Corp Callable 5% 2030ComplexBondCall schedule at par after Y2EOD clean price
Amortising MBS 4% 2035ComplexBondMonthly payment scheduleEOD price
Convertible 2.5% 2027ComplexBondConverts to equity at 25:1EOD price, equity ref price
Zero Coupon UST 2028BondDiscount accretionEOD discount price
5Y SOFR IRSInterestRateSwapFixed 4% vs SOFR quarterlySOFR discount curve
6M USD Term DepositTermDepositFixed rate, maturity eventNone (face value)
📚
Understanding Fixed Income Instrument Types
Business Context for Implementation Consultants

Clients hold a variety of fixed income instruments, each with different cashflow characteristics. Understanding these is essential for correctly modelling them in LUSID and explaining to clients why the native definitions matter.

Vanilla bonds pay a fixed coupon on a regular schedule (semi annually or annually). The coupon rate, day count convention, and payment frequency determine exactly when and how much cash the bondholder receives. LUSID uses FlowConventions to model this schedule and can generate coupon events automatically.

Floating rate notes (FRNs) pay a variable coupon tied to a reference rate (e.g. SOFR + 150bps). The rate resets periodically (typically quarterly). LUSID needs IndexConvention to know which rate to reference and when resets occur.

Zero coupon bonds pay no coupons. They are issued at a discount and redeemed at par at maturity. The "return" is the difference between purchase price and par value (discount accretion).

Key implementation lesson: A bond with properties (coupon rate stored as metadata) is NOT the same as a bond with a native definition (FlowConventions, principal, start/maturity dates). Only the native definition enables LUSID to calculate accrued interest, generate coupon events, and value the bond correctly.

Learn more:

Investopedia Reference
Investopedia: Bonds Investopedia: Floating Rate Notes Investopedia: Zero Coupon Bonds Investopedia: Callable Bonds Investopedia: Convertible Bonds Investopedia: Mortgage Backed Securities Investopedia: Interest Rate Swaps Investopedia: Day Count Conventions
Reusing EDM+ Instruments
This notebook also references existing instruments from the EDM+ pack by their LusidInstrumentId (LUID). A handful of equities, the FX forwards, and the equity options are pulled into the IBOR narrative so you are working with a genuinely multi asset portfolio. No need to recreate what already exists.
✅ Verification Checkpoint

LUSID Web App → DATA MANAGEMENT → Instruments: Search by scope ibor-training-v8. You should see 158 instruments across 7 asset types:

Click any complex bond instrument to see its FlowConventions, coupon schedule, and maturity date in the instrument detail panel. Click the IRS to see both legs with their conventions and the SOFR IndexConvention.

Luminesce: Run SELECT DisplayName, Type, LusidInstrumentId FROM Lusid.Instrument WHERE Scope = 'ibor-training-v8' and confirm all instruments appear with the correct types.

Luminesce: Run SELECT * FROM Lusid.Instrument.Quote WHERE QuoteScope = 'ibor-training-v8-quotes' LIMIT 20 to confirm SOFR fixings, EOD prices, and pool factors are loaded.

2

NB01: Portfolio Structure & Entity Hierarchy

Build the portfolio master for your IBOR
📁
Why Portfolio Structure Matters

In a real implementation, the portfolio hierarchy defines how positions are aggregated, how compliance rules are scoped, and how reporting is structured. Getting this wrong means rebuilding later. Base currency cannot change after portfolio creation. Sub holding keys cannot be removed once transactions reference them. This notebook teaches you to get it right first time.

🏗️
Portfolio Hierarchy Design
Business Context

A well designed portfolio hierarchy is the foundation of every LUSID implementation. Getting it wrong means rebuilding later — base currency cannot change after creation, and sub holding keys cannot be removed once transactions reference them.

Transaction portfolios hold the actual investment positions. Each has a base currency, optional sub holding keys (for position segmentation by strategy, desk, or custodian), and links to corporate action sources for lifecycle event processing.

Portfolio groups aggregate multiple portfolios for compliance checking, reporting, and performance measurement. A single portfolio can belong to multiple groups.

Derived portfolios inherit positions from a parent portfolio and apply currency overlays or filters. Common use case: USD and GBP share class views of a multi currency fund.

Implementation rule: Always design the portfolio hierarchy with the client before creating anything in LUSID. The key decisions are: What are the reporting dimensions (strategy, desk, custodian)? These become sub holding keys. What aggregation views are needed? These become portfolio groups.

Learn more:

Investopedia Reference
Investopedia: Portfolio Investopedia: Share Classes
LUSID Knowledge Base
KB: Create a Transaction Portfolio KB: Derived Portfolios KB: What is a Scope?
▶️ Run NB01

In JupyterHub, open NB01_Portfolio_Structure.ipynb, select the Python (SDK v3) kernel, then click Run → Run All Cells.

Creates 8 portfolios, portfolio groups, derived portfolios, and enables lifecycle event processing. Note: You will see "recipe not found" errors for InstrumentEventConfiguration. This is expected — the recipe is created later in NB06. The lifecycle events will start working after NB06 completes.

NB01 Portfolio Structure & Entity Hierarchy
Creates the complete portfolio master: transaction portfolios, portfolio groups, derived portfolios for share class views, and portfolio property enrichment.
  1. Create 4 transaction portfolios: Global Equity, Global Fixed Income, Multi Asset, and Cash Management
  2. Configure sub holding keys (Strategy, CustodianAccount) on each portfolio
  3. Create portfolio groups: "All Portfolios", "InScope_Compliance", "Fixed Income Only"
  4. Create derived portfolios for USD and GBP share class views of the Multi Asset portfolio
  5. Enrich portfolios with properties (PortfolioManager, InceptionDate, Benchmark, InvestmentStrategy)
  6. Verify via Luminesce and LUSID web app
✅ Verification Checkpoint

LUSID Web App → DATA MANAGEMENT → Portfolios: Filter by scope ibor-training-v8. You should see 8 transaction portfolios (IBOR-EQ, IBOR-FI, IBOR-MA, IBOR-CASH, IBOR-SP500, IBOR-AITECH, IBOR-BLKC, IBOR-GAGG) + 2 derived portfolios (IBOR-MA-USD, IBOR-MA-GBP) + 3 portfolio groups (IBOR-ALL, IBOR-EQUITY-GROUP, IBOR-FI-GROUP). Click any portfolio to confirm sub holding keys and properties.

Luminesce: Run SELECT PortfolioCode, PortfolioType, BaseCurrency, SubHoldingKeys FROM Lusid.Portfolio WHERE PortfolioScope = 'ibor-training-v8'

3

NB02: Transaction Type Configuration

Configure the movements engine that drives your holdings
⚙️
The Movements Engine

Every transaction in LUSID generates one or more "movements" that debit or credit holdings. A Buy transaction, for example, credits the instrument holding and debits the cash holding. The movement definitions are configured per transaction type, and this configuration is what makes LUSID's holdings engine work. If the movements are wrong, every position downstream is wrong. This notebook teaches you the mechanics.

Critical lesson: Test movements in a UAT scope before promoting to production. A misconfigured movement definition on a Buy transaction will silently produce incorrect holdings for every portfolio that uses it.
▶️ Run NB02

In JupyterHub, open NB02_Transaction_Type_Configuration.ipynb, select the Python (SDK v3) kernel, then click Run → Run All Cells.

Configures lifecycle event transaction types: BondCoupon, SwapCashFlow, TermDeposit, Maturity, and futures/options.

NB02 Transaction Type Configuration
Configures transaction types, sides (Transaction vs Settlement), and movement definitions. Covers the standard deployment set plus custom types for bond lifecycle events.
  1. Review the existing default transaction types in the domain
  2. Create custom transaction types: BondCouponReceived, SwapCashflow, TermDepositMaturity
  3. Configure movement definitions for each type (which holdings are debited/credited)
  4. Create sides configuration (Transaction side vs Settlement side date handling)
  5. Verify via Lusid.TransactionType Luminesce provider
✅ Verification Checkpoint

Navigate to Data Virtualisation → Query Editor (see instructions above). In the query editor, run:

SELECT AliasType, AliasDescription, MovementTypes
FROM Lusid.TransactionType
WHERE AliasSource = 'default'
  AND AliasType IN ('BondCoupon', 'BondPrincipal', 'Maturity', 'SwapCashFlow',
                     'TermDepositInterest', 'TermDepositPrincipal',
                     'CashSettledOptionExercise', 'Expiry', 'FutureCashSettlement')
ORDER BY AliasType

You should see 9 custom lifecycle transaction types with their movement definitions (CashCommitment, StockMovement, Carry, CashReceivable).

KB Article
Input and Output Transactions
Input transactions (API loaded) vs output transactions (system generated from lifecycle events).
KB Article
Transaction Type Configuration
Movement definitions, sides, and the relationship between transaction types and holdings.
4

NB03: Transaction Loading (Securities & Cash)

Load the transaction history that drives your positions
💱
Transaction Processing & the Movements Engine
Business Context

Every transaction in LUSID generates "movements" that debit or credit holdings. A Buy credits the instrument holding and debits cash. A Sell does the reverse. The movement definitions are configured per transaction type and are what makes LUSID's holdings engine work.

Multi currency handling is critical. When a USD based portfolio buys a GBP denominated bond, the transaction must specify the FX rate (TradeToPortfolioRate) so LUSID can calculate the correct cash impact in the portfolio's base currency.

Transaction properties (broker, strategy, desk, trade reference) are enrichment metadata that flow through to holdings and reporting. They are the primary mechanism for tagging trades for downstream analysis.

Bond transaction pricing: Bond prices are quoted per 100 face value. A price of 99.25 on a $1,000,000 face value bond means the total consideration is $992,500. LUSID expects the price as quoted (99.25) and the units as face value (1,000,000). With unitised bonds (principal=1), the units field represents the number of units held.

Learn more:

Investopedia Reference
Investopedia: Transactions Investopedia: Settlement Date Investopedia: Trade Date
LUSID Knowledge Base
KB: Input and Output Transactions KB: Transaction Type Configuration
▶️ Run NB03

In JupyterHub, open NB03_Transaction_Loading.ipynb, select the Python (SDK v3) kernel, then click Run → Run All Cells.

Loads all transactions across all portfolios: equities, bonds, OTC, cash, multi asset, and the 4 iShares fund portfolios.

NB03 Transaction Loading
Loads securities transactions (buys, sells), cash transactions (FundsIn, FundsOut, coupon receipts), bond purchases, IRS bookings, and term deposit placements. Covers multi currency handling with FX rate calculations and transaction property enrichment.
  1. Load equity buy/sell transactions across Global Equity portfolio
  2. Load bond purchase transactions (the fully defined bonds from NB00) into Global Fixed Income
  3. Load IRS booking and term deposit placement into Multi Asset portfolio
  4. Load cash transactions: FundsIn (initial capital), FundsOut (distributions), dividend receipts
  5. Load multi currency transactions with explicit FX rates (USD, GBP, EUR positions)
  6. Enrich transactions with properties: Broker, Strategy, Desk, TradeRef
  7. Verify via GetTransactions API and Lusid.Portfolio.Txn Luminesce provider
✅ Verification Checkpoint

LUSID Web App → DATA MANAGEMENT → Portfolios:

  1. Filter by scope ibor-training-v8
  2. Right click on any portfolio code (e.g. IBOR-FI)
  3. Select View Transactions
  4. Confirm transactions are visible with correct amounts, currencies, and properties (Strategy, CustodianAccount, Broker)
  5. Check that bond transaction prices are decimal (e.g. 0.9925 not 99.25)
Additional Training: Create a Holdings Chart

Use the built in charting to visualise holdings by strategy:

  1. Right click IBOR-FI → View Holdings
  2. Set effective date to 30 Sep 2024
  3. Click the Chart icon in the toolbar (bar chart icon)
  4. In the chart setup panel on the right:
    • Categories: select Strategy from the dropdown
    • Aggregate: toggle OFF
    • Series: select Units
  5. You should see a bar chart showing holdings grouped by investment strategy (InvestmentGrade, FloatingRate, StructuredCredit, Convertibles, HighYield)

This demonstrates how LUSID's Sub Holding Keys enable instant portfolio segmentation without any additional configuration.

Luminesce: Run SELECT PortfolioCode, TransactionId, Type, Units, TotalConsideration, TransactionCurrency FROM Lusid.Portfolio.Txn WHERE PortfolioScope = 'ibor-training-v8'

5

NB04: Holdings & Position Management

See LUSID's on demand holdings engine in action
📊
On Demand Holdings

This is where the IBOR training gets interesting. Unlike legacy systems that run a nightly batch to compute positions, LUSID calculates holdings on demand from the full transaction history. You can ask for a holding at any effective date and any as at timestamp. The result always reflects the complete, auditable transaction chain. This notebook is where you see that principle in practice.

📊
On Demand Holdings & Bitemporal Data
Business Context

Traditional systems run a nightly batch to compute end of day positions. LUSID calculates holdings on demand from the complete transaction history. This means you can ask for a holding at any effective date (when did the event happen?) and any as at timestamp (when did we know about it?). This is the bitemporal data model.

Position types tell you the nature of each holding: P (Physical) for securities, B (Balance) for cash, C (Commitment) for unsettled trades, F (Forward) for future dated positions.

Sub holding key breakdowns show the same instrument split by different dimensions. For example, 1,500 shares of AAPL might break down as 1,000 in CoreEquity strategy and 500 in GrowthEquity strategy.

Why this matters to clients: Bitemporal holdings mean you can always answer "What did we think our position was at close of business last Tuesday?" even if a late booking was entered the next day. This is essential for regulatory reporting, audit trails, and performance attribution.

Learn more:

Investopedia Reference
Investopedia: Holdings Investopedia: Position
LUSID Knowledge Base
KB: What is Bitemporal Data? KB: IBOR Portfolio Management
▶️ Run NB04

In JupyterHub, open NB04_Holdings_Position_Management.ipynb, select the Python (SDK v3) kernel, then click Run → Run All Cells.

Queries holdings across all portfolios, demonstrates bitemporal queries and SHK breakdowns.

NB04 Holdings & Position Management
Demonstrates GetHoldings at various effective dates, position types (P, B, C, F), sub holding key breakdowns, set holdings vs adjust holdings patterns, and the difference between input and output transactions.
  1. Call GetHoldings for the Global Equity portfolio at today's effective date
  2. Call GetHoldings at a historical effective date (before a sell transaction) to see the position difference
  3. Query holdings with sub holding key breakdown (positions by Strategy, by CustodianAccount)
  4. Demonstrate SetHoldings pattern for tactical migration from a legacy system
  5. Call GetHoldings on the Fixed Income portfolio after a coupon date and observe the coupon cash receipt from the fixed rate bonds
  6. Observe the floating rate reset event on the FRN and the adjusted notional on the amortising bond
  7. Compare GetTransactions (input only) vs BuildTransactions (includes system generated output transactions from lifecycle events)
  8. Verify via Lusid.Portfolio.Holding Luminesce queries
The "Aha" Moment
When you call GetHoldings at different effective dates, the positions change dynamically based on the transaction history. Compare holdings before and after the equity sell transaction to see the bitemporal engine in action. For lifecycle events (coupon payments, floating rate resets), NB05 demonstrates how to configure and process these via corporate actions and instrument events.
✅ Verification Checkpoint

LUSID Web App → DATA MANAGEMENT → Portfolios: Navigate to scope ibor-training-v8, right click on the IBOR-FI (Global Fixed Income) portfolio and select View Holdings. Set the effective date to any date within the January 2024 to June 2025 range (recommended: 2024-09-30). You should see 7 bond positions (type P) and cash balances (type B) from the initial funding and bond purchases. Note: automatic coupon lifecycle events from FlowConventions require InstrumentEventConfiguration to be set on the portfolio. NB05 covers lifecycle events explicitly via corporate actions and instrument events.

Luminesce: Run SELECT DisplayName, Units, CostAmount, CostCurrency, HoldingType FROM Lusid.Portfolio.Holding WHERE PortfolioScope = 'ibor-training-v8' AND PortfolioCode = 'IBOR-FI'

KB Article
IBOR Portfolio Management
GetHoldings, BuildTransactions, position types (P, B, C, F), holding views.
6

NB05: Corporate Actions & Lifecycle Events

Automate the investment lifecycle
🔄
Corporate Actions & the Instrument Lifecycle
Business Context

Corporate actions are events initiated by a company that affect its securities: stock splits, dividends, mergers, conversions, rights issues. They are one of the most operationally complex areas of investment management because they change positions without a trade being executed.

Stock splits increase the number of shares outstanding while proportionally reducing the price. A 2:1 split doubles your shares and halves the price. Your total market value stays the same but your position records must update.

Cash dividends generate cash income from equity holdings. They create a new cash position on the payment date without any transaction being loaded.

Bond lifecycle events (coupon payments, floating rate resets, principal paydowns, maturity events) are generated automatically by LUSID when instruments carry full native definitions. This requires InstrumentEventConfiguration on the portfolio.

Implementation complexity: Corporate actions are the single most common source of reconciliation breaks. A missed stock split doubles your position error. A missed dividend creates a phantom cash discrepancy. Always verify corporate action processing as part of UAT sign off.

Learn more:

Investopedia Reference
Investopedia: Corporate Actions Investopedia: Stock Splits Investopedia: Dividends Investopedia: Ex-Date
LUSID Knowledge Base
KB: Corporate Actions in LUSID
🔄
Corporate Actions & Lifecycle Events
Business Context

Corporate actions change positions without a trade: stock splits double shares, dividends generate cash, mergers swap instruments. They are the most common source of reconciliation breaks.

Automatic lifecycle events (bond coupons, FRN resets, maturity) are generated by LUSID from instrument definitions when InstrumentEventConfiguration is set on the portfolio.

Implementation risk: A missed 2:1 stock split doubles your position error. A missed dividend creates a phantom cash break. Always verify corporate action processing during UAT.
Corporate Actions Stock Splits Dividends Ex Date
LUSID Knowledge Base
KB: Corporate Actions
▶️ Run NB05

In JupyterHub, open NB05_Corporate_Actions.ipynb, select the Python (SDK v3) kernel, then click Run → Run All Cells.

Creates the AAPL 2:1 stock split and verifies lifecycle events (bond coupons, IRS cashflows, term deposit maturity).

NB05 Corporate Actions & Lifecycle Events
Creates a 2:1 AAPL stock split via legacy corporate actions. The lifecycle event verification (bond coupons, IRS cashflows, term deposit maturity) is in the NB06 section below, since the valuation recipe must be created first.
  1. Create a corporate action source and link it to portfolios
  2. Create and process a stock split event (2:1) on AAPL
  3. Verify holdings before and after the split date
  4. Verify IRS swap cashflows and term deposit lifecycle via BuildTransactions
▶️ Run NB06

In JupyterHub, open NB06_Valuation_Recipe.ipynb, select the Python (SDK v3) kernel, then click Run → Run All Cells.

Creates the valuation recipe with 5D quote interval lookback, enables InstrumentEventConfiguration on portfolios, then runs valuations across all 7 portfolios.

NB06 Valuation & Recipe Configuration
Creates a valuation recipe with a market data rules and 5D quote interval lookback, runs valuations across all portfolios, and demonstrates accrued interest, FX conversion, and model based pricing for the IRS.
  1. Create a valuation recipe with market data rules: ClientInternal Price, RIC Price, RIC Rate (for SOFR fixings), FX CurrencyPair
  2. Configure pricing model rules: simple quote lookup for equities, clean price + accrued for bonds, discounted cashflow for IRS
  3. Run GetValuation on the Global Equity portfolio and review equity market values
  4. Run GetValuation on the Global Fixed Income portfolio and review bond valuations with accrued interest
  5. Run GetValuation on the Multi Asset portfolio and review cross asset results including IRS NPV
  6. Compare valued positions to expected results (manual calculation check)
  7. Verify via Lusid.Portfolio.Valuation Luminesce queries
✅ Verification Checkpoint

Navigate to Portfolio Management → Valuations in the left sidebar:

  1. Select scope ibor-training-v8
  2. Select recipe IBOR-VALUATION-RECIPE
  3. Set effective date to 30 Sep 2024
  4. Select portfolio IBOR-FI (Global Fixed Income) and run the valuation
  5. Confirm market values appear for all 7 bond holdings plus cash
  6. The fixed rate bonds should show accrued interest. The zero coupon bond should show discount accretion
  7. Now switch to IBOR-EQ (Global Equity) and run the valuation
  8. Confirm all 10 equity positions show market values based on the loaded price quotes
  9. Check the GBP denominated positions (Shell, AstraZeneca) are converted to USD using the FX rates
Verification Checkpoint

LUSID Web App → DATA MANAGEMENT → Portfolios → [Any Portfolio] → Valuation: Select the ibor-training-v8 recipe and run a valuation. Confirm market values appear for all holdings.

Key check: The fixed rate bonds should show accrued interest as a separate line. The IRS should show a net present value (positive or negative depending on rate movements). The zero coupon bond should show discount accretion.

Data Enrichment & Lifecycle Events Reference

Beyond the basic instruments and trades, this training pack carries a rich layer of operational data and a full set of lifecycle events. This section documents what was added so you can demonstrate and verify each item.

Enriched transaction properties

Every equity, fixed income and multi-asset trade carries operational properties used across front, middle and back office workflows. These are visible in the web app on a portfolio's Transactions view via the column picker, and queryable in Luminesce via Lusid.Portfolio.Txn.Property.

PropertyExampleUsed for
StrategyCoreEquity, GrowthEquity, IncomeEquitySub-holding key; performance attribution
CustodianAccountCUST-BNY-001, CUST-JPM-002, CUST-STATE-003Sub-holding key; custodian reconciliation
Broker / CounterpartyNameGoldman Sachs, UBS, BarclaysCounterparty exposure, commission analysis
ExecutionVenue / PlaceOfTradeNYSE, NASDAQ, CBOE, LSEBest-execution and venue analysis
OrderId / AllocationIdORD-20240201-0001Order-to-trade lineage
SettlementStatus / ConfirmationStatusSettled, ConfirmedMiddle-office settlement tracking
CommissionAmount / TaxAmount105.47 USD / 168.0 GBPCost and tax reporting

Sub-holding keys (SHK)

Why valuations must group by SHK

The portfolios use two sub-holding keys, Strategy and CustodianAccount. An instrument held under two different SHK buckets (for example AAPL bought into both CUST-BNY-001/CoreEquity and CUST-JPM-002/CoreEquity) occupies two holding lines. When valuing, group by instrument name and Holding/default/SubHoldingKey; grouping by instrument name alone causes multi-bucket instruments to collapse to a null result and understates the portfolio.

OTC instruments modelled as SimpleInstrument

The interest rate swap (IBOR-IRS-5Y-SOFR), term deposit (IBOR-TD-6M-USD) and zero coupon bond (IBOR-ZCB-2028) are created as SimpleInstrument rather than their native types. Native InterestRateSwap and TermDeposit types invoke LUSID's pricing models, which override quote-based pricing and can produce unrealistic valuations; the native zero-coupon Bond is rejected because it has a zero payment frequency. As SimpleInstruments they price purely from the monthly mark-to-market quotes loaded in NB00, which is the appropriate approach for a training environment.

FX coverage

FX quotes are loaded for all currency pairs the iShares portfolios require (TWD, HKD, CNY, JPY, KRW, EUR, GBP, CHF, SEK, DKK, AUD, CAD, ILS, INR, SGD, BRL against USD), in both slash (TWD/USD) and dot (TWD.USD) formats and as both Price and Rate quote types. The valuation recipe carries an Fx.*.* rule with suppliers={"Fx": "Client"} so non-USD holdings resolve to the portfolio base currency.

Futures & options lifecycle events

NB05 books the full expiry and settlement lifecycle for the derivatives, contrasting in-the-money exercise (cash settlement) against out-of-the-money expiry (worthless). Verify these on the IBOR-MA Transactions view (set Transaction status to All transactions) or via the NB08 F&O lifecycle query.

InstrumentEvent typeDateOutcome
IBOR-TYZ4 (10Y UST future)FutureCashSettlement2024-12-19Settled at final price
IBOR-ESZ4 (S&P 500 E-mini future)FutureCashSettlement2024-12-20Settled at final price
IBOR-AAPL-C200 (call, deep ITM)CashSettledOptionExercise2024-12-20Exercised for cash (intrinsic value)
IBOR-AMZN-P220 (put, ITM)CashSettledOptionExercise2024-12-20Exercised for cash
IBOR-GOOGL-C190 (call, ATM)CashSettledOptionExercise2024-12-20Exercised for cash
IBOR-CLF5 (WTI crude future)FutureCashSettlement2025-01-21Settled at final price
IBOR-GCG5 (gold future)FutureCashSettlement2025-02-26Settled at final price
IBOR-NQH5 (NASDAQ 100 future)FutureCashSettlement2025-03-21Settled at final price
IBOR-NVDA-C150 (call, deep ITM)CashSettledOptionExercise2025-03-21Exercised for cash
IBOR-MSFT-P380 (put, OTM)Expiry2025-03-21Expired worthless (no cash)
IBOR-META-C600 (call, OTM)Expiry2025-03-21Expired worthless (no cash)

Corporate action

An AAPL 2:1 stock split (ex-date 2024-08-01) is applied via the corporate action source IBOR-CA-SOURCE-V3 attached to IBOR-EQ. The verification below shows the holding doubling across the split date.

Verifying the Stock Split

How to Verify the Stock Split in the Web App

Verify the stock split by comparing IBOR-EQ holdings before and after the split date:

  1. Go to Data Management → Portfolios, filter by scope ibor-training-v8
  2. Right click IBOR-EQ → View Holdings
  3. Set effective date to 31 Jul 2024 (before the split)
  4. Note the AAPL units (e.g. 2,000 + 200 across two custodian lines)
  5. Change effective date to 02 Aug 2024 (after the split)
  6. AAPL units should have doubled (e.g. 4,000 + 400)
  7. The cost basis remains the same (cost per share halved, total cost unchanged)

Note: Corporate action adjustments are applied directly to holdings, not as separate visible transactions. LUSID applies the 2:1 units factor and 1:1 cost factor at the ex date (2024-08-01).

Verifying Bond Coupon Events

How to Verify Bond Coupons in the Web App

Navigate to the Fixed Income holdings to see coupon income accumulating:

  1. Go to Data Management → Portfolios
  2. Filter by scope ibor-training-v8
  3. Right click IBOR-FI (Global Fixed Income) → View Holdings
  4. Set effective date to 30 Sep 2024
  5. Find the CustodianAccount column header
  6. Drag the CustodianAccount column header upwards into the grouping area to group holdings by custodian
  7. Under CUST-BNY-001, click on the USD Cash Balance row (~$22M)
  8. You should see bond coupon payments automatically generated by LUSID from the FlowConventions
  9. These were never manually loaded. LUSID generated them via InstrumentEventConfiguration

Verifying IRS Swap Cashflows & Term Deposit Lifecycle

How to Verify in the Web App

Check IBOR-MA holdings at different dates to see lifecycle events:

  1. Right click IBOR-MA → View Holdings
  2. Set effective date to 2024-12-31
  3. You should see multiple CCY_USD cash balance lines with different amounts from swap cashflows and TD interest
  4. Change to 2025-03-31 (after TD maturity on 2025-01-01)
  5. The term deposit holding should have disappeared and cash should include returned principal ($5M)
📈
IRS Swap Cashflow Timeline
System Generated Events from SOFR Fixings
DateEventAmountSource
2024-09-01SwapCashFlow (Receive, Leg 2)+$135,444SOFR quarterly reset at 5.35%
2024-12-01SwapCashFlow (Receive, Leg 2)+$131,444SOFR quarterly reset at 5.20%
2025-01-01TermDepositInterest+$135,4445.3% on $5M for 184 days
2025-01-01TermDepositPrincipal+$5,000,000Principal return at maturity
2025-01-01Maturityn/aRemoves term deposit holding
2025-03-01SwapCashFlow (Receive, Leg 2)+$122,500SOFR quarterly reset at 4.90%
2025-06-01SwapCashFlow (Pay, Leg 1)($400,000)Fixed leg annual payment (4%)
2025-06-01SwapCashFlow (Receive, Leg 2)+$121,389SOFR quarterly reset at 4.75%
Training point: None of these cashflows were manually loaded. LUSID generated every one from the IRS instrument definition and the SOFR rate fixings in the Quote Store.
▶️ Run NB07

In JupyterHub, open NB07_Reconciliation.ipynb, select the Python (SDK v3) kernel, then click Run → Run All Cells.

Creates custodian mirror portfolio, runs holdings reconciliation, and sets up Group Reconciliation for the UI.

NB07 Reconciliation Framework
Creates a custodian side derived portfolio, loads mock custodian positions, configures break codes and group reconciliation definitions, runs the recon, then deliberately introduces breaks to demonstrate the detection framework.
  1. Create a "Custodian" scope with a derived portfolio representing the custodian's view
  2. Load custodian position data (mock data matching IBOR positions)
  3. Configure break code data type with standard break definitions (UnitBreak, CashBreak, PriceBreak, MissingInIBOR, MissingInCustodian)
  4. Create a group reconciliation definition linking IBOR portfolios to custodian portfolios
  5. Run the reconciliation — should show zero breaks (clean match)
  6. Deliberately delete one transaction from IBOR and re run the recon to see a UnitBreak
  7. Deliberately modify an FX rate and re run to see a CashBreak
  8. Review break results and categorisation
  9. Verify via Lusid.Portfolio.Reconciliation Luminesce queries
✅ Verification Checkpoint

Luminesce: Run the reconciliation query and confirm: (1) initial run shows zero breaks, (2) after deleting a transaction, a UnitBreak appears for that instrument, (3) after modifying an FX rate, a CashBreak appears for the affected currency position.

KB Article
Luminesce Generic Reconciliation Provider
SQL based reconciliation with custom metrics, tolerance rules, and multi portfolio grouping.
9

Web App Configuration & UI Training

Hands on exercises in the LUSID web application
How to View Reconciliation Results

Navigate to Reconciliations → Valuation in the left sidebar:

  1. Left side: Portfolio Global Fixed Inco... (IBOR-FI-FLAT), Recipe IBOR-VALUATION-RE..., Effective 30 Sep 2024
  2. Right side: Portfolio Custodian Positio... (CUSTODIAN-MIRROR), Recipe IBOR-VALUATION-RE..., Effective 30 Sep 2024
  3. Currency: Default (USD) for both sides
  4. Click the Unmatched tab to see the Convertible 2.5% March 2027 (exists in IBOR but missing from custodian)
  5. Click Partially matched (8) to see all paired positions with their PV differences
  6. Look for the Amortising MBS row which shows the valuation impact of the 50,000 unit break
  7. Click All (9) and tick Display diffs to see the complete picture

The screenshot shows the Unmatched (1) tab with the Convertible 2.5% March 2027. This position exists in IBOR-FI-FLAT but is missing from the custodian mirror, valued at $1,107,244.

The Valuation reconciliation view shows the most complete picture, including market value differences alongside unit breaks.

  1. Navigate to Reconciliations → Valuation
  2. Left side: Portfolio IBOR-FI-FLAT, Recipe IBOR-VALUATION-RECIPE, Effective 30 Sep 2024
  3. Right side: Portfolio CUSTODIAN-MIRROR, Recipe IBOR-VALUATION-RECIPE, Effective 30 Sep 2024
  4. Tick the Display diffs checkbox
  5. You should see:
    • Partially matched (8): Instruments where both sides have positions, showing PV differences
    • Unmatched (1): Convertible 2.5% March 2027 (exists in IBOR but missing from custodian)
    • MBS break: Diff (PV Sum) of ~$5.3M from the 50,000 unit discrepancy
    • Cash break: ~$1M difference in USD cash reflecting the cost impact of the missing positions
🔧
Fixing the Breaks
Training Exercise

After identifying the breaks, the trainee should fix them in LUSID and re-run the reconciliation:

BreakCauseFix
MBS Unit Break
IBOR: 5,000,000
Custodian: 4,950,000		 Missed principal paydown of 50,000 face value Load a Sell transaction of 50,000 units on IBOR-AMORT-MBS-2035 into the IBOR-FI portfolio, or apply a BondPrincipal corporate action. Then re-flatten IBOR-FI-FLAT and re-run the recon.
Missing Convertible
IBOR: 1,000,000
Custodian: 0		 Custodian did not report the position (either a reporting error or the bond was called/converted) Contact the custodian. If the bond was called, load a BondEarlyRedemption corporate action. If it is a reporting error, no IBOR action needed.
Training point: Reconciliation is iterative. Fix the break, re-flatten, re-run, verify the break resolves. In the web app, LUSID shows the result status as "Resolved" when a previously broken position now matches.
Holdings Reconciliation (Position Level Breaks)

For a detailed position level view showing unit and cost breaks, navigate to Reconciliations → Holdings:

  1. In the left sidebar, expand Reconciliations
  2. Select Holdings (not Valuation)
  3. Left side: Portfolio IBOR-FI-FLAT (Global Fixed Inco...), Recipe IBOR-VALUATION-RECIPE, Effective 30 Sep 2024
  4. Right side: Portfolio CUSTODIAN-MIRROR (Custodian Positio...), Recipe IBOR-VALUATION-RECIPE, Effective 30 Sep 2024
  5. Tick Display diffs
  6. Click Unmatched tab to see the breaks:

You should see 3 unmatched items:

  • Amortising MBS 4% May 2035: IBOR has 5,000,000 units, Custodian has 4,950,000 — Diff of 50,000 units (missed principal paydown)
  • USD Cash: Diff of $10,587,500 reflecting the cost impact of the MBS break and missing Convertible
  • GBP Cash: Diff of $2,000,000 from the GBP funding not in the custodian mirror

The Matched (5) tab shows the 5 bonds that agree between IBOR and custodian.

The red highlighted cells show the differences. The MBS Diff (Units) of 50,000 and Diff (Cost) of 50,000 confirm the principal paydown break.

Fixing the MBS Break (Training Exercise)

To resolve the 50,000 unit MBS break, load a Sell transaction for 50,000 units into the Custodian Positions (Mirror) portfolio:

  1. Navigate to Portfolio Management → Transactions
  2. Select scope ibor-training-v8, portfolio CUSTODIAN-MIRROR
  3. Click + Add Transaction
  4. Set the fields:
    • Transaction type: Buy [default]
    • Instrument: Amortising MBS 4% May 2035
    • Quantity: 50,000
    • Price: 100
    • Transaction CCY: USD
    • Settlement CCY: USD
  5. Click Save

After saving, navigate back to Reconciliations → Holdings with the same settings (IBOR-FI-FLAT vs CUSTODIAN-MIRROR, 30 Sep 2024) and confirm the MBS break has resolved. The MBS row should move from Unmatched to Matched.

Training point: In production, this fix would be a principal paydown transaction loaded via an automated custodian feed or a manual booking after investigation. The reconciliation workflow is: identify break → investigate cause → load correcting transaction → re-run recon → confirm resolution.

Why UI Proficiency Matters
Business Context

Implementation Consultants need to demonstrate LUSID's web application to clients fluently. The API and SDK are for automation, but clients interact with the web app daily for valuations, position monitoring, manual trade entry, and ad hoc reporting. These exercises build the muscle memory needed for confident client demos.

Demo rule: Always rehearse in the web app before a client session. Know where every menu item is, how to switch effective dates, and how to explain what the client is seeing. Fumbling with the UI during a demo destroys confidence.
How to Access the Luminesce Query Editor

Several exercises and verification steps use Luminesce SQL queries. To access the Query Editor:

  1. In the LUSID web app, click Data Virtualisation in the left sidebar
  2. Expand the submenu and click Query Editor
  3. You should see the query input area at the top and results grid at the bottom
  4. On the right panel, the Catalogue shows available data providers:
    • Lusid — Portfolio, Instrument, Quote, TransactionType providers
    • Sys — System tables (Registration, Field)
    • Drive — File storage providers
    • Tools — Utility functions
  5. Type your SQL query in the editor, then click Run (or press Shift+Enter)
  6. Use Save to store frequently used queries

Menu path: Data Virtualisation → Query Editor

Luminesce Showcase Queries

Copy any query below into Data Virtualisation → Query Editor and click Run. These demonstrate how to join instrument, transaction, and holding properties to build rich reports from the enriched data set.

Query 1: Equity Security Master

All equity instruments with identifiers, GICS, ESG, and fundamentals:

SELECT
    i.DisplayName as [Name], i.ClientInternal,
    p1.Value as [ISIN], p2.Value as [Sector], p3.Value as [Country],
    p4.Value as [ESG Rating], p5.Value as [Market Cap], p6.Value as [Beta],
    p7.Value as [P/E Ratio], p8.Value as [Settlement Cycle], p9.Value as [Index Membership]
FROM Lusid.Instrument i
    LEFT JOIN Lusid.Instrument.Property p1 ON p1.InstrumentScope = 'ibor-training-v8' AND p1.PropertyCode = 'Isin' AND p1.LusidInstrumentId = i.LusidInstrumentId
    LEFT JOIN Lusid.Instrument.Property p2 ON p2.InstrumentScope = 'ibor-training-v8' AND p2.PropertyCode = 'Sector' AND p2.LusidInstrumentId = i.LusidInstrumentId
    LEFT JOIN Lusid.Instrument.Property p3 ON p3.InstrumentScope = 'ibor-training-v8' AND p3.PropertyCode = 'Country' AND p3.LusidInstrumentId = i.LusidInstrumentId
    LEFT JOIN Lusid.Instrument.Property p4 ON p4.InstrumentScope = 'ibor-training-v8' AND p4.PropertyCode = 'ESGRating' AND p4.LusidInstrumentId = i.LusidInstrumentId
    LEFT JOIN Lusid.Instrument.Property p5 ON p5.InstrumentScope = 'ibor-training-v8' AND p5.PropertyCode = 'MarketCap' AND p5.LusidInstrumentId = i.LusidInstrumentId
    LEFT JOIN Lusid.Instrument.Property p6 ON p6.InstrumentScope = 'ibor-training-v8' AND p6.PropertyCode = 'Beta' AND p6.LusidInstrumentId = i.LusidInstrumentId
    LEFT JOIN Lusid.Instrument.Property p7 ON p7.InstrumentScope = 'ibor-training-v8' AND p7.PropertyCode = 'PriceEarningsRatio' AND p7.LusidInstrumentId = i.LusidInstrumentId
    LEFT JOIN Lusid.Instrument.Property p8 ON p8.InstrumentScope = 'ibor-training-v8' AND p8.PropertyCode = 'SettlementCycle' AND p8.LusidInstrumentId = i.LusidInstrumentId
    LEFT JOIN Lusid.Instrument.Property p9 ON p9.InstrumentScope = 'ibor-training-v8' AND p9.PropertyCode = 'IndexMembership' AND p9.LusidInstrumentId = i.LusidInstrumentId
WHERE i.Scope = 'ibor-training-v8' AND i.Type = 'Equity'
ORDER BY i.DisplayName LIMIT 50
Query 2: Trade Blotter with Lifecycle Fields

Transactions with counterparty, venue, settlement details, and commission (change IBOR-EQ to any portfolio):

SELECT
    t.PortfolioCode as [Portfolio], t.TransactionId as [Trade ID], t.Type,
    t.InstrumentName as [Instrument], t.Units, t.TransactionPrice as [Price],
    t.TotalConsideration as [Consideration], t.TransactionCurrency as [CCY],
    t.TransactionDate as [Trade Date], t.SettlementDate as [Settle Date],
    p1.Value as [Counterparty], p2.Value as [Venue], p3.Value as [Broker],
    p4.Value as [Settlement Method], p5.Value as [Commission], p6.Value as [Trade Source]
FROM Lusid.Portfolio.Txn t
    LEFT JOIN Lusid.Portfolio.Txn.Property p1 ON p1.PortfolioScope = 'ibor-training-v8' AND p1.PortfolioCode = t.PortfolioCode AND p1.TransactionId = t.TransactionId AND p1.PropertyCode = 'CounterpartyName'
    LEFT JOIN Lusid.Portfolio.Txn.Property p2 ON p2.PortfolioScope = 'ibor-training-v8' AND p2.PortfolioCode = t.PortfolioCode AND p2.TransactionId = t.TransactionId AND p2.PropertyCode = 'ExecutionVenue'
    LEFT JOIN Lusid.Portfolio.Txn.Property p3 ON p3.PortfolioScope = 'ibor-training-v8' AND p3.PortfolioCode = t.PortfolioCode AND p3.TransactionId = t.TransactionId AND p3.PropertyCode = 'Broker'
    LEFT JOIN Lusid.Portfolio.Txn.Property p4 ON p4.PortfolioScope = 'ibor-training-v8' AND p4.PortfolioCode = t.PortfolioCode AND p4.TransactionId = t.TransactionId AND p4.PropertyCode = 'SettlementMethod'
    LEFT JOIN Lusid.Portfolio.Txn.Property p5 ON p5.PortfolioScope = 'ibor-training-v8' AND p5.PortfolioCode = t.PortfolioCode AND p5.TransactionId = t.TransactionId AND p5.PropertyCode = 'CommissionAmount'
    LEFT JOIN Lusid.Portfolio.Txn.Property p6 ON p6.PortfolioScope = 'ibor-training-v8' AND p6.PortfolioCode = t.PortfolioCode AND p6.TransactionId = t.TransactionId AND p6.PropertyCode = 'TradeSource'
WHERE t.PortfolioScope = 'ibor-training-v8' AND t.PortfolioCode = 'IBOR-EQ'
ORDER BY t.TransactionDate DESC LIMIT 30
Query 3: Enriched Holdings Report

Holdings joined with instrument properties (change portfolio and date as needed):

SELECT
    h.PortfolioCode as [Portfolio], h.InstrumentName as [Instrument],
    h.Units, h.Cost as [Cost Basis], h.Currency,
    p1.Value as [Sector], p2.Value as [Country], p3.Value as [ESG Rating], p4.Value as [ISIN]
FROM Lusid.Portfolio.Holding h
    LEFT JOIN Lusid.Instrument.Property p1 ON p1.InstrumentScope = 'ibor-training-v8' AND p1.PropertyCode = 'Sector' AND p1.LusidInstrumentId = h.LusidInstrumentId
    LEFT JOIN Lusid.Instrument.Property p2 ON p2.InstrumentScope = 'ibor-training-v8' AND p2.PropertyCode = 'Country' AND p2.LusidInstrumentId = h.LusidInstrumentId
    LEFT JOIN Lusid.Instrument.Property p3 ON p3.InstrumentScope = 'ibor-training-v8' AND p3.PropertyCode = 'ESGRating' AND p3.LusidInstrumentId = h.LusidInstrumentId
    LEFT JOIN Lusid.Instrument.Property p4 ON p4.InstrumentScope = 'ibor-training-v8' AND p4.PropertyCode = 'Isin' AND p4.LusidInstrumentId = h.LusidInstrumentId
WHERE h.PortfolioScope = 'ibor-training-v8' AND h.PortfolioCode = 'IBOR-SP500'
    AND h.EffectiveAt = '2024-09-30' AND h.HoldingType = 'P'
ORDER BY h.Cost DESC LIMIT 30
Query 4: Cross Portfolio Summary

Aggregated position count and total cost per portfolio:

SELECT
    h.PortfolioCode as [Portfolio], COUNT(*) as [Positions],
    SUM(h.Cost) as [Total Cost], h.Currency as [Base CCY]
FROM Lusid.Portfolio.Holding h
WHERE h.PortfolioScope = 'ibor-training-v8' AND h.EffectiveAt = '2024-09-30' AND h.HoldingType = 'P'
GROUP BY h.PortfolioCode, h.Currency
ORDER BY SUM(h.Cost) DESC
Query 5: F&O Lifecycle Events

All futures/options expiry, settlement, and exercise transactions:

SELECT
    t.TransactionId as [Event ID], t.Type as [Event Type],
    t.InstrumentName as [Instrument], t.Units,
    t.TransactionPrice as [Settlement Price], t.TotalConsideration as [P&L],
    t.TransactionDate as [Date]
FROM Lusid.Portfolio.Txn t
WHERE t.PortfolioScope = 'ibor-training-v8' AND t.PortfolioCode = 'IBOR-MA'
    AND t.Type IN ('FutureCashSettlement', 'CashSettledOptionExercise', 'Expiry')
ORDER BY t.TransactionDate

Tip: Click Save in the Query Editor to store any of these for reuse. The key patterns are: LEFT JOIN Lusid.Instrument.Property for instrument enrichment, LEFT JOIN Lusid.Portfolio.Txn.Property for transaction lifecycle data, and GROUP BY for portfolio summaries.

id="nb08-ex1">Exercise 1: Create a Valuation Dashboard

Ex.1 Single Portfolio Valuation
  1. Navigate to DASHBOARDS → Valuations
  2. Click Create Dashboard (or the + button)
  3. Select scope ibor-training-v8, portfolio IBOR-EQ
  4. Select recipe: scope ibor-training-v8, code IBOR-VALUATION-RECIPE
  5. Set effective date to 2024-09-30 (primary valuation date for training data)
  6. Click Run — you should see all 10 equities with market values
📸
Screenshot: IBOR-EQ Valuation Dashboard
Show the valuation table with all 10 equities, market values, and the effective date selector
✅ Verify

All 10 equities visible (AAPL, MSFT, NVDA, JPM, JNJ, XOM, PG, V, SHEL, AZN) with varying quantities. Cash balances in USD and GBP. Market values should look reasonable (e.g. AAPL ~1,500 × ~$180 = ~$270,000).

Demo Tip
Change the effective date to 2024-03-31 then 2025-03-31 to show how the valuation changes with different market data. This demonstrates LUSID's bitemporal pricing without any code.

Exercise 2: Create a Dashboard Set

Ex.2 Multi Portfolio Dashboard Set
  1. From the Valuations area, click Dashboard Sets or Create Set
  2. Add three dashboards: IBOR-EQ, IBOR-FI, and IBOR-MA
  3. All using recipe ibor-training-v8/IBOR-VALUATION-RECIPE
  4. Name the set: IBOR Training: All Portfolios
  5. Set effective date to 2024-09-30 and run
📸
Screenshot: Dashboard Set with all three portfolios
Show the tab/navigation allowing switching between IBOR-EQ, IBOR-FI, and IBOR-MA
Demo Tip
Dashboard sets are how portfolio managers monitor their entire book. Each portfolio can use a different recipe (e.g. one uses Bloomberg prices, another uses internal marks).

Exercise 3: Create a Pie Chart (Holdings by Sector)

Ex.3 Sector Breakdown Visualisation
  1. Open the IBOR-EQ valuation dashboard
  2. Look for a Charts or Visualisation option in the toolbar
  3. Select Pie Chart as the chart type
  4. Group by: Instrument/ibor-training-v8/Sector
  5. Measure: Valuation/PV/Amount (market value)
📸
Screenshot: Pie chart showing sector breakdown
Technology should be largest (AAPL + MSFT + NVDA), followed by Financials, Energy, Healthcare, Consumer Staples
Demo Tip
Properties drive all reporting dimensions. If a client wants breakdown by country, asset class, or credit rating, it is just a property definition away. No schema changes needed.

Exercise 4: Customise Holdings View (Add Columns)

Ex.4 Add Custom Columns to Holdings
  1. Navigate to DATA MANAGEMENT → Portfolios → scope ibor-training-v8
  2. Right click IBOR-EQ → View Holdings
  3. Set effective date to 2024-09-30
  4. Click the Columns or gear icon to configure the view
  5. Add columns: Instrument/ibor-training-v8/Sector, Holding/default/Currency, Holding/default/Cost
  6. Drag column headers to reorder
📸
Screenshot: Holdings view with Sector, Currency, and Cost columns added
Show the customised column layout with property values populated
Demo Tip
Column configuration is per user. Properties from any domain (Instrument, Transaction, Portfolio) can be added as columns. Each team can create their own views of the same data.

id="nb08-ex5">Exercise 5: Create a Saved View

Ex.5 Save & Share Column Configuration
  1. From the holdings view configured in Exercise 4
  2. Click Save View or Save As
  3. Name it: IBOR Equity Holdings (Full)
  4. Save, navigate away, then return and select the saved view
  5. Confirm all custom columns are restored
Demo Tip
Saved views are shared. A portfolio manager creates their view, and ops/compliance/reporting teams can use it too — or create their own.

Exercise 6: Add a Transaction via the UI

Ex.6 Manual Trade Entry
  1. Navigate to DATA MANAGEMENT → Portfolios → ibor-training-v8 → right click IBOR-EQ → View Transactions
  2. Click Add Transaction
  3. Enter: ID EQ-MANUAL-001, Type Buy, Instrument IBOR-AAPL
  4. Units: 100, Price: 200.00, Currency: USD
  5. Trade Date: 2024-10-15, Settlement Date: 2024-10-17
  6. Save and refresh holdings — AAPL units should increase by 100
📸
Screenshot: Add Transaction dialog
Show the trade entry form with all fields populated
Demo Tip
Manual entry is for corrections, late bookings, and OTC trades outside automated feeds. The UI enforces the same validation as the API.

Exercise 7: Add a Sub Holding Key

Ex.7 Position Segmentation with SHKs
  1. Navigate to DATA MANAGEMENT → Portfolios → Create Portfolio
  2. Scope: ibor-training-v8, Code: IBOR-EQ-SHK-TEST, Base Currency: USD
  3. Sub Holding Keys: add Transaction/ibor-training-v8/Strategy AND Transaction/ibor-training-v8/Broker
  4. Create the portfolio, then load a few test transactions with different Strategy and Broker values
  5. View holdings — the same instrument appears as separate rows when Strategy or Broker differs
📸
Screenshot: Holdings showing SHK breakdown
Same instrument appearing multiple times, segmented by Strategy and Broker
Important
SHKs must be set at portfolio creation and cannot be changed after transactions are loaded. Always confirm the segmentation dimensions with the client during design.

Exercise 8: Add an Instrument Property

Ex.8 Extend the Data Model
  1. Navigate to DATA MANAGEMENT → Properties
  2. Click Create Property Definition
  3. Domain: Instrument, Scope: ibor-training-v8, Code: CreditRating, Type: string
  4. Save the definition
  5. Navigate to instrument IBOR-UST-4.5-2029 and add value: AAA
  6. Repeat for other bonds: IBOR-CALL-5-2030 = BBB+, IBOR-CONV-2.5-2027 = BB
  7. Return to the holdings view and add CreditRating as a column
📸
Screenshot: Instrument detail page with CreditRating property
Show the property section on an instrument with the AAA rating applied
Demo Tip
Properties are the extensibility mechanism. Credit rating, ESG score, liquidity tier, regulatory classification — all without schema changes. Properties flow through to all reporting, compliance, and analytics.

id="nb08-ex9" style="padding-top:8px;">Exercise 9: Luminesce Table Joins

Ex.9 SQL Joins Across Data Providers
  1. Navigate to TOOLS → Query Editor (Luminesce)
  2. Paste the following query to join holdings with instrument data:
SELECT 
    h.PortfolioCode,
    h.LusidInstrumentId,
    h.Units,
    h.CostAmount,
    h.HoldingType,
    i.DisplayName,
    i.Type AS InstrType
FROM Lusid.Portfolio.Holding h
INNER JOIN Lusid.Instrument i 
    ON h.LusidInstrumentId = i.LusidInstrumentId 
    AND i.Scope = 'ibor-training-v8'
WHERE h.PortfolioScope = 'ibor-training-v8'
  AND h.PortfolioCode = 'IBOR-EQ'
  AND h.EffectiveAt = '2024-09-30'
ORDER BY h.Units DESC
LIMIT 20
📸
Screenshot: Luminesce query results showing joined data
Show the enriched results with both holdings data and instrument names/types

Additional query to try — cross portfolio cost summary:

SELECT 
    h.PortfolioCode,
    h.HoldingType,
    SUM(h.CostAmount) AS TotalCost
FROM Lusid.Portfolio.Holding h
WHERE h.PortfolioScope = 'ibor-training-v8'
  AND h.EffectiveAt = '2024-09-30'
  AND h.HoldingType = 'P'
GROUP BY h.PortfolioCode, h.HoldingType
Demo Tip
Luminesce is the reporting engine. Any data in LUSID (holdings, transactions, instruments, properties, legal entities) can be joined using standard SQL. This replaces custom ETL pipelines for building reporting databases.

Exercise 10: Create a Bar Chart

Ex.10 Fixed Income Market Value Chart
  1. Open the IBOR-FI valuation dashboard
  2. Create a Bar Chart showing market value by instrument
  3. Group by instrument name, measure by Valuation/PV/Amount
  4. Try switching between bar, line, and table views
  5. Change the effective date month by month to see how bond prices moved
📸
Screenshot: Bar chart showing FI portfolio market values
Show all 7 bonds with their market values as bars
✅ UI Training Complete

All 10 exercises completed. You can confidently demonstrate dashboards, charts, custom views, manual transactions, property management, and Luminesce reporting in the LUSID web app.

IBOR Readiness Checklist

CheckCriteriaVerified?
Instrument DefinitionsAll cashflow generating instruments have full native definitions with FlowConventions☐
Market DataSOFR fixings, EOD prices, SOFR fixings, and discount curves loaded☐
Portfolio StructurePortfolios, groups, derived portfolios, and SHKs configured☐
Transaction TypesAll custom transaction types have correct movement definitions☐
Transaction HistoryAll securities and cash transactions loaded with properties☐
Holdings GenerationGetHoldings returns expected positions at multiple effective dates☐
Lifecycle EventsBond coupons, FRN resets, and corporate actions generate correct output transactions☐
ValuationRecipe produces market values with accrued interest and model pricing☐
ReconciliationIBOR vs custodian recon shows zero breaks on clean data☐
Web App ProficiencyDashboards, charts, saved views, manual transactions, Luminesce joins all demonstrated☐
